title: How to Shop Animal Based at Costco

chunks.title: Introduction to Animal-Based Haul
chunks.summary: The video opens with an introduction to a shopping haul focused on animal-based foods available at Costco. The host expresses enthusiasm for finding organic fruits and mentions the various unhealthy snacks that they will avoid during their shopping trip.
chunks.transcript: Costco - I'm going to show you guys what I'm gonna get for an animal-based haul  even at Costco You can find lots of good animal-based foods. Come on Look at the size of this cart. Costco is crazy consumerism. I forgot how many things you had to walk through

chunks.title: Avoiding Processed Foods
chunks.summary: The host critiques processed foods and snacks, highlighting harmful ingredients like artificial flavors and added sugars. They emphasize the negative health implications of consuming such products and reflect on how these foods contribute to poor health.
chunks.transcript: Don't need any of that stuff. Let's see what we got here. Mix and match muffins. Definitely not. You know these are gonna have seed oils. We got some canola oil right there and a million ingredients. Corn starch, monocalcium phosphate, FDC, yellow number five, which I believe is tartrazine. Linked to hyperactivity in kids and irritable bowel syndrome. I think it's also been linked to inflammatory bowel disease and animal models. This is the kind of shit that we used to love as kids, but this didn't mean no good. I mean, look at xanth and gum. That wasn't good for my gut as a kid. Artificial flavors. I don't even need to read the label. How could anyone buy a food with this many things on the label? I don't think anyone actually thinks vanilla, chocolate chunk muffins are healthy, but it's scary. I mean, this is, I think, a big reason people are just not that healthy. There's too many foods like this that creep into the food supply.

chunks.title: Fresh Produce Selection
chunks.summary: In this segment, the host discusses their preferences for organic fresh produce over conventional options, particularly avoiding items that are heavily sprayed with pesticides. They mention specific fruits like grapes and strawberries that they opt not to buy.
chunks.transcript: Got a big thing of organic blueberries. They've got a bunch of grapes here, but I don't eat grapes unless they're organic. Grapes have a ton of pesticides. So I skipped the non-organic grapes, for sure. More vegetables. no thanks don't need any asparagus my pee smells fine as it is Brussels sprouts, field greens, pass. No, thanks. I'm gonna skip the mushrooms in case you guys haven't seen me talk about mushrooms. It's pretty clear that white button mushrooms like this contain compounds that are carcinogenic. A guerotine in white button mushrooms has been linked to bladder cancer and animal models. If you cook the white button mushrooms, you probably get rid of most of the carcinogens, but I still don't eat them because I'm not sure we can get rid of all the carcinogens just by cooking them. Chitake mushrooms don't have a guerotine, but mushrooms are still fungi. They're stuck in the ground. They have defensive chemicals. I'm not convinced there are any unique compounds in mushrooms that allow humans to be optimally healthy that you can't get in things like meat and organs, fruit, honey, and raw dairy on an animal-based diet. There's a compound in mushrooms that gets talked about sometimes called erogothionine. It's an amino acid that's been linked to improvements in cognitive function with aging, but guess what? You can also get erogothionine in liver. So skip the mushrooms. The corn, it's all GMO. It's gonna be full of glyphosate. That's all we need in here. I'm good on strawberries. I don't think they have any organic ones.

chunks.title: Meat Options at Costco
chunks.summary: The host arrives at the meat section and emphasizes the importance of selecting grass-fed meat over grain-finished options. They discuss the health benefits of grass-fed beef and lamb, including concerns about toxins found in grain-finished meats.
chunks.transcript: Ooh, cakes. No thanks. Skip that stuff. Oh, finally, all right, we got some meat. Let's see what we got here at Costco. The Promised Land, we have arrived. All right, organic ground beef. Of course, it's Costco, so you get three pounds, and they've got ground bison too. That's cool. I usually prefer to get the fattiest meat that I can. This bison is a little leaner than I like it, 90/10, but when I cook this ground bison, I'll probably add a little bit of fat in the form of tallow or butter, or maybe I'll make some bone marrow and eat that with the lean ground beef. Costco has a lot of USDA choice meat, which is grain finished. Let's see if I can find any grass fed. I'm not optimistic. Now, they don't have grass fed beef, but they do have a lamb leg from Australia, and I'm pretty sure that all of the lamb in Australia is entirely grass fed. I know that all the lamb in New Zealand is entirely grass fed, so I'm always trying to get grass fed, grass finished meat, because when you grain finished cattle, the cattle are getting grains with plastic, with microplastics, with PFAs, pyrofluoroalkylated compounds, in that grain is often moldy, and those mold toxins are gonna end up in the meat, along with the glyphosate that's sprayed on the grains. So in so many ways, I think that grass fed, grass finished beef or lamb is healthier than grain finished. I'm gonna skip the pork. If I could find truly pastured pork

chunks.title: Concerns Regarding Pork and Poultry
chunks.summary: The segment focuses on the host's thoughts about pork and chicken, expressing a preference for pastured meat over conventionally raised options due to their high linoleic acid content. They provide insights on how these fatty acids affect health.
chunks.transcript: Pork is great. It's higher in thymine from the, according to the USDA, but you can get thymine from fruit and plant foods and orange juice, if you're worried about that. My problem with most pork is that it's fed grains. It's fed corn and soy, and that corn and soy is very high in linoleic acid, and pigs and chickens can't get rid of that linoleic acid, which means the fat of pigs and the fat of chickens fed corn and soy ends up much higher in linoleic acid than the corresponding fat of a wild pig or a wild chicken. There's literature like this to suggest that in wild chickens and wild pigs, the amount of linoleic acid is around 5%, 5% or 6%, but in domesticated chickens and pigs, you're looking at 15%, 16%, 17% linoleic acid,

chunks.title: Dairy Choices
chunks.summary: The host discusses their preference for raw dairy products, explaining the health benefits compared to pasteurized options. They also note the misleading nature of non-dairy products labeled as milk alternatives.
chunks.transcript: but I wanna show you guys soon. They've at least got organic milk, so that would be the place that I would start, but I prefer raw milk. They did have raw cheese, and I think it's kind of hilarious that they have the oat milk and the almond milk and the soy milk in the dairy container, even though these are not dairy at all, trying to pretend, whatever. It's freezing in here. They've got some heavy whipping cream over there that's organic. That would be pretty good with some honey or maple syrup. It's not raw, but if I didn't have access to raw dairy, that's what I would get. All right, let's get out of here, it's freezing. I don't eat a lot of seafood because heavy metals, microplastics, PFAS, per-fluoroalkylated substances, but if you want to eat seafood, make sure you get wild salmon that is Alaskan sockeye. Don't get Atlantic salmon. All Atlantic salmon is farm raised, and if you're getting shrimp or shellfish, be aware that some shrimp, I bet we can find some over there, has added colorings in it that you do not want. You just want an uncolored shrimp that you can cook yourself. Let's see if we can find some cheese here. They've got this Gruyere from Switzerland. It's pretty easy to find. Aged A-plus months made with raw milk. You can see here that when a cheese is not raw, even though it's organic, it's not raw, you can see on the label, it says that the milk is pasteurized. So pasteurized means the milk has been heated 160, 200 degrees. That seems to change the confirmation of the whey protein in the milk. You can see from studies like this one, the kids who drink raw milk have lower rates of asthma, allergies, and eczema in adulthood. There seems to be something protective about the whey protein that is undenatured in raw milk and probably in raw cheese. So I always opt for raw dairy when I can get it. How about that?

chunks.title: Seafood Selection
chunks.summary: The host shares guidance on choosing seafood, specifically recommending wild Alaskan salmon and cautioning against farmed varieties due to heavy metal contamination. They also highlight the importance of avoiding colored shrimp.
chunks.transcript: because heavy metals, microplastics, PFAS, per-fluoroalkylated substances, but if you want to eat seafood, make sure you get wild salmon that is Alaskan sockeye. Don't get Atlantic salmon. All Atlantic salmon is farm raised, and if you're getting shrimp or shellfish, be aware that some shrimp, I bet we can find some over there, has added colorings in it that you do not want. You just want an uncolored shrimp

chunks.title: Final Thoughts on Animal-Based Shopping
chunks.summary: Concluding the video, the host summarizes their purchases, highlighting the variety of quality animal-based foods available at Costco. They encourage viewers to consider their shopping choices and emphasize the importance of avoiding seed oils.
chunks.transcript:
The animal-based run. We got some ground bison, some ground beef. We got some lamb that's probably grass-fed. You got some organic fruit. You got some raw cheese, some avocados. Yeah, not bad. I mean, even at Costco, you can find super solid, really high-quality animal-based foods. Even here, you can find it. So this is how I would shop at Costco. Thanks for watching the video. "No seed oils"
